title: Discharging a capacitor
summary: This lesson details the process of a capacitor discharging through a resistor. It covers the exponential decay of charge, potential difference, and current, introduces the concept of the time constant (τ = RC), and explores the energy transformations involved. Essential equations and graphical methods for analysis are explained with worked examples.
learningObjectives: Describe the exponential discharge of a capacitor and sketch graphs of p.d., charge, and current against time.
Define the time constant (τ = RC) and recall and use the decay equations: x = x₀e⁻ᵗ/ᴿᶜ.
Solve problems involving the discharge of a capacitor, including calculations of time, p.d., charge, and energy.
Analyse graphical data of capacitor discharge to determine the time constant.

simpleExplanation.title: The Capacitor's Slow Fade
simpleExplanation.summary: When a charged capacitor is connected to a resistor, it doesn't empty instantly. It discharges exponentially, with its voltage, charge, and current all decreasing over time at a predictable rate.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a full bucket of water (the charged capacitor) with a small hole at the bottom connected to a narrow pipe (the resistor). The water level (voltage) doesn't drop at a constant rate. It falls quickly at first when the pressure is high, then more and more slowly as the bucket empties. The size of the bucket (capacitance) and the narrowness of the pipe (resistance) determine how long it takes to drain.
steps: A charged capacitor stores energy; discharge through a resistor.
Capacitor p.d. V decays exponentially: V = V₀e^(−t/RC).
Time constant τ = RC — time to fall to ~37% of V₀.
Energy dissipates in the resistor as the capacitor discharges.

flashcards.front: What is the equation for the potential difference V across a discharging capacitor at time t?
flashcards.back: $V = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$, where $V_0$ is the initial p.d., R is the resistance, and C is the capacitance.

flashcards.front: What is the equation for the charge Q remaining on a discharging capacitor at time t?
flashcards.back: $Q = Q_0 e^{-t/RC}$, where $Q_0$ is the initial charge.

flashcards.front: What is the equation for the current I in the circuit during capacitor discharge at time t?
flashcards.back: $I = I_0 e^{-t/RC}$, where $I_0$ is the initial current ($I_0 = V_0/R$).

flashcards.front: Define the time constant, τ, for a capacitor-resistor circuit.
flashcards.back: The time constant, τ, is the product of resistance and capacitance (τ = RC). It is the time taken for the charge, p.d., or current to fall to $1/e$ (approximately 37%) of its initial value.

flashcards.front: What is the initial current $I_0$ when a capacitor with initial p.d. $V_0$ discharges through a resistor R?
flashcards.back: The initial current is $I_0 = V_0 / R$. This is the maximum current during the discharge process, occurring at t=0.

flashcards.front: How is the half-life ($t_{1/2}$) of a capacitor discharge related to the time constant (τ)?
flashcards.back: The half-life is the time taken for the charge/p.d. to halve. $t_{1/2} = RC \ln(2) \approx 0.693 RC$. So, $t_{1/2} \approx 0.693τ$.

flashcards.front: How can you determine the time constant from a graph of p.d. against time?
flashcards.back: Find the time on the x-axis that corresponds to the p.d. dropping to $0.37 \times V_0$. Alternatively, draw a tangent to the curve at t=0; the tangent will intersect the time axis at t = τ.

flashcards.front: How can you obtain a straight-line graph from the exponential decay equation $V = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$?
flashcards.back: Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $\ln(V) = \ln(V_0) - t/RC$. A graph of $\ln(V)$ against $t$ is a straight line with gradient $-1/RC$ and y-intercept $\ln(V_0)$.

flashcards.front: What happens to the energy stored in the capacitor as it discharges?
flashcards.back: The electrical potential energy stored in the capacitor is transferred to thermal energy in the resistor as charge flows through it, causing it to heat up.

flashcards.front: A common mistake is to assume the p.d. halves in a time of τ/2. Why is this wrong?
flashcards.back: Discharge is exponential, not linear. The rate of discharge is constantly decreasing. The time to halve is $t_{1/2} = τ \ln(2) \approx 0.693τ$, which is more than half the time constant.

flashcards.front: How does the energy stored in a discharging capacitor change with time?
flashcards.back: Energy $E = E_0 e^{-2t/RC}$. The energy decays at twice the rate of voltage or charge, as it is proportional to $V^2$ or $Q^2$.

faq.q: Why is the current maximum at the start of the discharge?
faq.a: At the very beginning (t=0), the potential difference across the resistor is equal to the full initial p.d. of the capacitor, $V_0$. According to Ohm's Law ($I=V/R$), this maximum voltage results in the maximum current, $I_0 = V_0/R$. As the capacitor discharges, V decreases, so the current I must also decrease.

faq.q: What is the difference between the time constant (τ) and the half-life (t₁/₂)?
faq.a: The time constant, $τ = RC$, is the time for a quantity (V, Q, or I) to fall to $1/e$ (approx 37%) of its initial value. The half-life, $t_{1/2}$, is the time for the quantity to fall to 50% of its initial value. They are related by $t_{1/2} = τ \ln(2) \approx 0.693τ$. The half-life is always shorter than the time constant.

faq.q: Does the capacitor ever fully discharge to zero?
faq.a: Mathematically, the exponential decay function $e^{-t/RC}$ only approaches zero as $t$ approaches infinity. It never actually reaches zero in a finite amount of time. However, for all practical purposes, a capacitor is considered fully discharged after about 5 time constants ($5τ$), as the remaining charge is less than 1% of the original charge.

faq.q: How does the energy stored in the capacitor change during discharge?
faq.a: The energy stored in a capacitor is given by $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$. Since $V = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$, the energy at time $t$ is $E = \frac{1}{2}C(V_0 e^{-t/RC})^2 = (\frac{1}{2}CV_0^2)e^{-2t/RC}$. This means the energy stored, $E$, also decays exponentially, but with a time constant of $RC/2$, i.e., twice as fast as the voltage or charge.

sections.content: In the previous topic, we explored how to charge a capacitor, storing energy within its electric field. Now, we investigate the reverse process: what happens when we provide a path for this stored energy to be released? This lesson focuses on the discharge of a capacitor through a resistor, a fundamental process in electronics with applications ranging from camera flashes to timing circuits.
The Discharge Process: An Exponential Decay

Imagine a capacitor fully charged to a potential difference $V_0$, holding a charge $Q_0$. If we connect this capacitor across a resistor $R$, a path is created for the electrons to flow from the negative plate to the positive plate. This flow of charge constitutes a current.

At the very first moment ($t=0$), the p.d. across the resistor is at its maximum, $V_0$, so the initial current is also at its maximum, given by Ohm's law: $I_0 = V_0 / R$. As charge flows, $Q$ on the plates decreases. Since $V = Q/C$, the p.d. $V$ across the capacitor also decreases. This, in turn, reduces the current ($I = V/R$). Because the current is decreasing, the rate at which charge leaves the capacitor also slows down. This self-regulating slowdown results in an exponential decay, where the quantities $Q$, $V$, and $I$ decrease rapidly at first, and then more and more slowly, theoretically never reaching zero in finite time.

items: Discharge occurs when a charged capacitor is connected in a closed circuit with a resistor.
Electrons flow from the negative plate to the positive plate, neutralising the stored charge.
The current, potential difference, and charge all decrease over time.
The rate of discharge is greatest at the beginning and decreases as the capacitor discharges.

sections.content: The exponential nature of capacitor discharge can be described by a set of precise mathematical equations. These are derived from solving the differential equation for the circuit, but for A-Level Physics, you need to know and be able to apply them. The variable $x$ can represent potential difference ($V$), charge ($Q$), or current ($I$).

Here, $x_0$ is the initial value of the quantity at $t=0$, $t$ is the time elapsed, $R$ is the resistance, $C$ is the capacitance, and $e$ is the base of the natural logarithm (approximately 2.718). Specifically:

- For potential difference: $V = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$
- For charge: $Q = Q_0 e^{-t/RC}$
- For current: $I = I_0 e^{-t/RC}$

sections.question:
A 470 μF capacitor is charged to a potential difference of 12.0 V. It is then discharged through a 2.20 kΩ resistor. 
(a) Calculate the initial charge stored on the capacitor. 
(b) Calculate the potential difference across the capacitor after 2.00 s. 
(c) Calculate the time taken for the charge on the capacitor to fall to 25% of its initial value.
sections.solution:
Part (a): Calculate initial charge $Q_0$.
$Q_0 = C V_0$
$Q_0 = (470 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}) \times 12.0 \text{ V}$
$Q_0 = 5.64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ C} = 5.64 \text{ mC}$

Part (b): Calculate p.d. $V$ at $t = 2.00$ s.
First, calculate the time constant, $τ = RC$.
$τ = (2.20 \times 10^3 \text{ Ω}) \times (470 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}) = 1.034 \text{ s}$
Now use the discharge equation for voltage:
$V = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$
$V = 12.0 \times e^{-2.00 / 1.034}$
$V = 12.0 \times e^{-1.934}$
$V = 12.0 \times 0.1445 = 1.734 \text{ V}$
So, $V \approx 1.73 \text{ V}$ (to 3 s.f.)

Part (c): Calculate time $t$ for $Q$ to be $0.25 Q_0$.
We use the discharge equation for charge: $Q = Q_0 e^{-t/RC}$.
We are given $Q = 0.25 Q_0$.
$0.25 Q_0 = Q_0 e^{-t/RC}$
$0.25 = e^{-t/RC}$
Take the natural logarithm of both sides:
$\ln(0.25) = -t/RC$
$t = -RC \ln(0.25)$
$t = -(1.034 \text{ s}) \times (-1.386)$
$t = 1.433 \text{ s}$
So, $t \approx 1.43 \text{ s}$ (to 3 s.f.)

sections.content: The product $RC$ in the exponent has units of time and is a crucial parameter of the circuit. It is called the time constant, represented by the Greek letter tau, $τ$.

The time constant is a measure of how quickly the capacitor discharges. A larger time constant (from a larger resistor or a larger capacitor) means a slower discharge. After one time constant has passed ($t = τ$), the charge, p.d., and current will have fallen to $e^{-1}$ (or approximately 37%) of their initial values. After five time constants ($t = 5τ$), the capacitor is considered to be almost fully discharged, as the values will have fallen to less than 1% of their initial levels ($e^{-5} \approx 0.0067$).

Examiners frequently ask for the definition of the time constant. State that it is the product of resistance and capacitance, AND that it is the time for the charge/p.d. to fall to $1/e$ (or 37%) of its initial value. Both parts are needed for full marks. Also, be prepared to determine the time constant from a graph of $V$ vs $t$ by finding the time at which $V = 0.37 V_0$.

sections.question: A student plots a graph of $\ln(V/V)$ against time $t$ for a discharging capacitor and finds the gradient of the best-fit line to be $-0.125 \text{ s}^{-1}$. If the capacitor has a capacitance of 500 μF, what is the resistance of the resistor in the circuit?
sections.solution:
The equation for discharge is $V = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$.
To get a linear graph, we take natural logs:
$\ln(V) = \ln(V_0 e^{-t/RC})$
$\ln(V) = \ln(V_0) + \ln(e^{-t/RC})$
$\ln(V) = \ln(V_0) - \frac{t}{RC}$
This is in the form $y = c + mx$, where $y = \ln(V)$, $x = t$, the y-intercept $c = \ln(V_0)$, and the gradient $m = -\frac{1}{RC}$.

The student has plotted $\ln(V)$ against $t$. The gradient is given as $-0.125 \text{ s}^{-1}$.
Therefore, Gradient = $-1/RC = -0.125 \text{ s}^{-1}$.
$1/RC = 0.125$
$RC = 1 / 0.125 = 8.0 \text{ s}$. (This is the time constant, τ).

We are given $C = 500 \text{ μF} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$.
We need to find R.
$R = \frac{RC}{C} = \frac{8.0 \text{ s}}{500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}}$
$R = 16000 \text{ Ω} = 16 \text{ kΩ}$.

steps.caption: A charged capacitor stores energy; discharge through a resistor.
steps.embedHint: Charge the capacitor, then connect it through a resistor.

steps.caption: Capacitor p.d. V decays exponentially: V = V₀e^(−t/RC).
steps.embedHint: Plot voltage vs time — exponential decay, not linear.

steps.caption: Time constant τ = RC — time to fall to ~37% of V₀.
steps.embedHint: Increase R or C — decay becomes slower (larger τ).

steps.caption: Energy dissipates in the resistor as the capacitor discharges.
steps.embedHint: Compare heat/light in resistor branch while voltage falls.
